name: Applications of Trigonometry — Heights and Distances
whatIsIt: This is trigonometry put to work to measure things you can't reach — a tower's height, a river's width, a kite's height. You look up at an object along a slanting line of sight; the angle above the horizontal is the ANGLE OF ELEVATION. Looking DOWN, the angle below horizontal is the ANGLE OF DEPRESSION. Then a right triangle plus tan, sin or cos gives the missing height or distance.
whyNeeded: Surveyors, sailors, pilots and builders constantly need lengths that are impossible to measure directly. A single angle taken with an instrument, plus one known distance, unlocks the answer with tan θ = opposite ÷ adjacent.
realLifeUses: Finding a tower or tree's height from its shadow and the sun's angle
Measuring the width of a river from one bank
A pilot judging distance to the runway (angle of depression)
Working out how high a kite flies from the string angle

vocabulary.meaning: The angle above the horizontal when you look UP at an object.
The angle below the horizontal when you look DOWN at an object.
The straight line from your eye to the object you look at.
opposite ÷ adjacent — the ratio used most for heights and distances.

story.title: The Temple Tower's Shadow
story.text: At the temple, the tower casts a shadow 20 m long, and the sun is at an angle of elevation of 45°. 'How tall is the tower?' Kavya asks. Thatha draws a right triangle: the tower is the opposite side, the shadow the adjacent. tan 45° = height ÷ 20, and tan 45° = 1, so height = 20 × 1 = 20 m. 'When the sun is at 45°, a thing's shadow equals its height!' Kavya beams.
story.extractedProblem: A tower casts a 20 m shadow when the sun's elevation is 45°. Find the tower's height.
story.answerInStory: 20 m — tan 45° = height ÷ 20, and tan 45° = 1, so height = 20.

visual.caption: The tower (opposite), the shadow (adjacent) and the line of sight make a right triangle: tan θ = height ÷ distance.

teachingGallery.title: Draw the right triangle first
teachingGallery.note: Mark the horizontal, the vertical height, and the angle of elevation; then pick tan = opposite/adjacent.

shapes.label: angle of depression from a cliff

examples.caption: Looking down, the angle of depression equals the elevation from the boat (alternate angles).
Height is opposite the angle; the ground distance is adjacent — that's why tan fits.

standardMethod.summary: Draw a right triangle, label the height (opposite) and ground distance (adjacent), and use tan θ = opposite ÷ adjacent (or sin/cos if the slant side is involved) to find the unknown.
steps: Sketch the situation as a right triangle; mark the angle of elevation or depression.
Label which side is opposite the angle (usually the height) and which is adjacent (the ground distance).
Choose the ratio: tan for opposite/adjacent, sin for opposite/hypotenuse, cos for adjacent/hypotenuse.
Put in the known angle value (tan 45° = 1, tan 60° = √3, tan 30° = 1/√3) and solve.
Check the answer is sensible (a tower taller than a person, etc.).

workedExamples.problem: A tower casts a 20 m shadow at a 45° angle of elevation. Find its height.
From the top of a cliff, a boat has an angle of depression of 45°. The cliff is 50 m tall. How far is the boat from the base?
steps: Angle of depression 45° equals the angle of elevation from the boat.

commonMistakes.mistake: Mixing up elevation (looking up) and depression (looking down).
commonMistakes.fix: Elevation is measured UP from the horizontal; depression is measured DOWN. They are equal alternate angles, so the triangle is the same.

commonMistakes.mistake: Putting the height as adjacent instead of opposite the angle.
commonMistakes.fix: The height is OPPOSITE the angle of elevation; the ground distance is adjacent. tan θ = opposite ÷ adjacent = height ÷ distance.

easy.q: What is the value of tan 45°?
hintLadder: At 45° the two short sides are equal.

easy.q: The angle you look UP at the top of a tower is called the angle of elevation. Its size when your line of sight is horizontal (straight ahead) is how many degrees?
hintLadder: Horizontal means not tilted up at all.

medium.q: A 30 m cliff, boat at 45° depression. Distance = 30 ÷ tan 45° = 30 ÷ 1 = ?

challenge.q: Elevation 45°, ground distance 100 m to a tower. Height = 100 × tan 45° = ?
Ravi labels the tower's height as ADJACENT to the elevation angle. What's the fix?

options.label: The height is OPPOSITE the angle; use tan = height ÷ distance

challenge.answer: The height is OPPOSITE the angle; use tan = height ÷ distance
hintLadder: Stand at the ground angle and look up.

questions.q: Shadow 40 m, sun's elevation 45°. Tower height = 40 × tan 45° = ?
A 25 m lighthouse, boat at 45° depression. Distance to boat = 25 ÷ tan 45° = ?
Looking DOWN from a tower at a car, the angle is the angle of…

teachBackPrompt: Stand a pencil upright for a grown-up and shine a torch at 45°; show that its shadow equals its height because tan 45° = 1. Explain angle of elevation (looking up) versus depression (looking down) and how tan θ = height ÷ distance finds a tower's height.

revisionCard.summary: Angle of elevation = looking up; angle of depression = looking down (they are equal). Draw a right triangle: height is OPPOSITE the angle, ground distance is ADJACENT. Use tan θ = height ÷ distance. Handy: tan 45° = 1, tan 60° = √3, tan 30° = 1/√3.

rememberIt.hook: Angle of elevation looks UP; depression looks DOWN
rememberIt.unpack: Draw the right triangle, mark the angle, then use SOH-CAH-TOA. tan is the workhorse: tan(angle) = height / distance.

studentQuestions.a: Elevation = you look UP to the object (top of a tower). Depression = you look DOWN (cliff to a boat). Both measured from the horizontal.

studentQuestions.q: Which ratio usually solves height problems?
studentQuestions.a: Usually tan, because you know the ground distance and want the height: tan(angle) = opposite/adjacent = height/distance.

formulas.name: Height from distance
formulas.remember: Use tan when you know the ground distance
